objectives: Describe and compare Von Neumann and Harvard architectures, including the modified/hybrid form used in modern CPUs
Explain the differences between CISC and RISC processors and where each is used
Apply knowledge of GPUs, multicore and parallel processing to justify a processor choice for a given workload

retrieval.q: What is the key feature of the Von Neumann architecture?
retrieval.a: A single shared memory and bus for both data and instructions.

retrieval.q: Which architecture can fetch an instruction at the same time as reading/writing data, and why?
retrieval.a: Harvard, because it has separate memories and buses for instructions and data.

retrieval.q: In a CISC processor, where is the complexity of executing instructions mainly handled?
retrieval.a: In the hardware (microcode), whereas RISC pushes complexity to the software/compiler.

retrieval.q: From 1.1.1, why does adding more cores not always improve performance?
retrieval.a: Only if the task can be parallelised and the software supports it; sequential or dependent tasks gain little.

retrieval.q: From 1.1.1, what does the width of the address bus determine?
retrieval.a: The maximum number of addressable memory locations, calculated as 2^n.

keywords.def: Architecture with one shared memory and bus for both data and instructions; simple and flexible but prone to a bottleneck.
Architecture with separate memories and buses for data and instructions, allowing simultaneous fetch and data access.
Hybrid used in modern CPUs: unified main memory but separate L1 instruction and data caches.
Complex Instruction Set Computer; many complex variable-length instructions with complexity in hardware; used in x86 desktops.
Reduced Instruction Set Computer; few simple fixed-length instructions aiming for one cycle each; used in ARM mobile/embedded.
Many-core processor that applies the same operation to large blocks of data in parallel (data parallelism).
General-purpose computing on a GPU for non-graphics tasks such as machine learning, simulation and hashing.
Carrying out multiple computations simultaneously by splitting a task across cores.

content.heading: Von Neumann vs Harvard
points: Von Neumann uses one shared memory and bus for data and instructions; Harvard uses separate memories and buses
Von Neumann can suffer a bottleneck (cannot fetch an instruction and access data at once); Harvard can do both simultaneously
Von Neumann is simpler, cheaper and more flexible (general-purpose PCs); Harvard is more complex and costly (embedded, microcontrollers, DSPs)

content.heading: Modified (hybrid) Harvard
points: Modern CPUs combine both: a single unified main memory like Von Neumann
But separate L1 instruction and data caches like Harvard, close to the core
This gives some simultaneous-access benefit while keeping cost and flexibility manageable

content.heading: CISC vs RISC
points: CISC has many complex, variable-length, multi-cycle instructions with complexity in hardware; RISC has few simple, fixed-length instructions aiming for one cycle each
RISC is easier to pipeline and uses less power, making it ideal for battery devices
Typical use: CISC in x86 desktops/laptops; RISC in ARM mobile phones and embedded systems

content.heading: GPUs and parallel processing
points: A GPU has thousands of small cores applying the same operation to large data sets in parallel (data parallelism)
Uses include rendering pixels/vertices for 3D graphics and GPGPU tasks like ML training, simulation and crypto hashing
Multicore CPUs run several independent cores; tasks parallelise poorly with data dependency, coordination overhead or an inherently sequential algorithm (limited by the serial fraction)

worked_example.title: Choosing CPU vs GPU for a data-parallel workload
steps: Identify the workload: the same calculation applied to millions of independent data points with little branching
Recognise this as highly data-parallel, which suits many small cores working simultaneously
Match to a GPU: thousands of cores maximise throughput across the data points in parallel
Note the caveat: the software must be written to use the GPU, and very branchy/dependent code would suit a CPU
Conclude with justification: the GPU is the better choice here because it processes far more data points at once

task: Produce a comparison table for Von Neumann vs Harvard and CISC vs RISC, then write one or two sentences each on where each architecture is typically used and why.
challenge: A program sorts a list and then searches it, and the search cannot begin until the sort finishes. Explain, using the terms data dependency and serial fraction, why running this on a multicore CPU gives only limited speed-up.

exit_ticket.q: Why can a Harvard architecture be faster than Von Neumann?
exit_ticket.a: Separate memories/buses let it fetch an instruction and access data simultaneously, avoiding the Von Neumann bottleneck.

exit_ticket.q: Give one reason RISC is preferred in mobile phones.
exit_ticket.a: It uses lower power (simple fixed-length instructions), extending battery life; it is also easier to pipeline.

exit_ticket.q: Why is a GPU faster than a CPU for rendering 3D graphics?
exit_ticket.a: It has thousands of small cores that process many pixels/vertices in parallel rather than sequentially.
